title: Applying SFT Loss Masking
concept: Configuring the loss calculation to compute gradients ONLY on the code output tokens, masking prompt tokens.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, what was the main focus of structuring instruction data?

options.text: Formatting datasets into system, user, and assistant segments.
Creating graphical user interfaces for code.
Testing the quality of code outputs.

exercises.explain: The previous lesson was about formatting datasets into three segments: system, user, and assistant.

cards.title: What is Loss Masking?
cards.body: Loss masking involves ignoring certain tokens in a loss calculation, so we only focus on the relevant parts of the output.

cards.title: Loss Calculation
cards.body: Loss calculation determines how well our model's predictions match the actual outputs. We want to improve this by focusing only on code output.

cards.title: Masking Like a Filter
cards.body: Think of loss masking like a screen filter; it allows only certain elements through while blocking others, making it easier to focus on the important parts.

cards.title: Example of Loss Masking
cards.body: If we have a sentence 'What is 2 + 2? It is 4.', we would only calculate loss for '4', ignoring 'What is 2 + 2?'

cards.title: Myth: All Tokens Matter Equally
cards.body: Some might think we need to calculate loss for every token, but we actually want to focus on the most relevant ones to improve training.

cards.title: Visualizing Loss Masking
cards.body: Here’s how loss masking works in practice.

exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of applying loss masking in a model's training?

options.text: To ignore irrelevant text and focus on specific outputs.
To calculate the input text's value.
To format the dataset for easier understanding.
To compare different models during training.

exercises.explain: We apply loss masking to ignore irrelevant text, allowing us to focus on the specific outputs we want to improve.

exercises.prompt: Loss masking is used to consider all parts of the input equally in the training process.
exercises.explain: False. Loss masking focuses only on specific outputs, ignoring parts of the input that are less relevant.

exercises.prompt: Loss masking helps us compute gradients only on the ______ tokens.
exercises.sentence: Loss masking helps us compute gradients only on the code output tokens.
exercises.explain: The correct answer is 'code output' because that specifies what we want to focus on during loss calculation.

exercises.prompt: Which statement about loss masking is correct?

options.text: It helps improve the model's efficiency by ignoring non-relevant tokens.
It requires us to change the dataset format.
It increases the time needed for training.

exercises.explain: It helps improve the model's efficiency by concentrating on the relevant parts of the data, increasing training effectiveness.

exercises.prompt: Why might we choose to apply loss masking during gradient calculation?

options.text: To prevent information from the entire dataset being counted equally.
To make the model faster by reducing its size.
To ensure all tokens are equally important during training.
To remove all input data from consideration.

exercises.explain: We apply loss masking to prevent information from the entire dataset being counted equally, allowing us to concentrate on the parts that truly improve the model's performance.
